Jaise slider aage jaayega, deforestation ka area badhta dikhega. ```python from folium.plugins import TimestampedGeoJson # Ek dummy GeoJSON bana rahe hain. # Real life mein tere paas Sentinel-2 ya Landsat (NASA ka satellite) se aaya hua data hoga. geo_json_data = { "type": "FeatureCollection", "features": [ { "type": "Feature", "geometry": {"type": "Point", "coordinates": [-62.21, -3.46]}, "properties": { "time": "2019-01-01", "style": {"color": "yellow"}, "popup": "2019: Minor loss" } }, { "type": "Feature", "geometry": {"type": "Point", "coordinates": [-62.25, -3.50]}, "properties": { "time": "2021-01-01", "style": {"color": "orange"}, "popup": "2021: Moderate loss" } }, { "type": "Feature", "geometry": {"type": "Point", "coordinates": [-62.15, -3.40]}, "properties": { "time": "2023-01-01", "style": {"color": "red"}, "popup": "2023: Severe loss!" } } ] } # TimestampedGeoJson ko map pe laga do TimestampedGeoJson( geo_json_data, period="P1Y", # 1 Year ka interval add_last_point=True, auto_play=True, loop=False, max_speed=10, loop_button=True, date_options="YYYY/MM/DD", time_slider_drag_update=True ).add_to(m) m.save("deforestation_timelapse.html") ``` > 🎯 **Key Insight:** Time-lapse sirf fancy nahi hota. Ye decision-making ka weapon hai. Agar tu government ko dikha raha hai ki 2019 se 2023 ke beech kaise jungle gayab hua, toh wo visually impact hota hai. Numbers aur tables se log so jate hain, animation se wo chauk jate hain. Ye "Baadal Hatao, Asli Scene Dekho" wale module ka climax hai. ### Step 5: Raster Overlay (Agar Tujhe Image Hi Dikhani Hai) Bhai, kabhi kabhi points nahi, pura satellite image hi map pe daalni padti hai. Jaise tu ne `Median Compositing` ki hai aur ek saaf image banayi hai jisme laal colour mein deforestation dikhta hai. Us image ko (GeoTIFF) map pe kaise daale? Folium direct GeoTIFF nahi padh sakta. Tujhe usko pehle tile format mein convert karna padega. Iska asli jugaad hai: `branca` aur `rasterio` ka combination, ya seedha Earth Engine ka API use karke image ka map ID nikalna. Kyunki humne `Earth Engine Account:` aur `Authentication:` pehle se set up kiya hua hai, hum GEE ka `ee.TileLayer` ko Folium mein daal sakte hain. Ek custom function bana lete hain iske liye, warna tu GEE ka official Python docs dekh ke rona suru kar dega. ```python import ee # Earth Engine initialize kar (Authentication wala step pichle chapters se) ee.Initialize() # Ek dummy image le rahe hain (NDVI loss wala area) # Maan lo ye tera processed image hai jisme loss areas highlight hain image = ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2") \ .filterDate('2023-01-01', '2023-12-31') \ .filterBounds(ee.Geometry.Point([-62.21, -3.46])) \ .median() # Visualization parameters. Yahan tune colors set kiye vis_params = { 'bands': ['SR_B4', 'SR_B3', 'SR_B2'], # RGB bands 'min': 0, 'max': 3000, 'gamma': 1.4 } # Map ID generate kar map_id_dict = ee.Image(image).getMapId(vis_params) # Folium tile layer bana ke add kar folium.raster_layers.TileLayer( tiles=map_id_dict['tile_fetcher'].url_format, attr='Map Data © Google Earth Engine', overlay=True, name='Landsat Composite 2023' ).add_to(m) folium.LayerControl().add_to(m) m.save("deforestation_raster_overlay.html") ``` Samjha? Tu GEE se image laaya, uska URL nikala, aur Folium mein as a background tile daal diya. Ab map pe tera processed satellite image aa gaya, aur upar se tere deforestation markers bhi dikh rahe hain. Ekdum James Bond level ka spy gadget ban gaya tera dashboard. > ☕ **Real Talk:** Bhai, yahan 80% log atak jaate hain. Kyunki coordinate system mismatch ho jaata hai. GEE default mein WGS84 deta hai, jo Folium ke liye perfect hai. Lekin agar tu local `Rasterio` se download kiya hua GeoTIFF daal raha hai, toh usko pehle reproject karna padega. Nahi toh tu map pe image dekhega, lekin wo 100 meter aage-peeche shift ho jaayegi. "Bhai marker toh jungle mein hai, image mein toh road dikh raha hai?" Ye error aayega. Dhyan se reproject kar. ## Aakhri Gyaan: Tu Ab Don Ban Chuka Hai Bhai, 7 chapter ho gaye. Tune shuru kiya tha ek `Python Setup:` se, aur aaj tu ek poora interactive dashboard bana raha hai jo satellite data uthata hai, baadal hataata hai (`Baadal Hatao, Asli Scene Dekho`), jungle ki health check karta hai (`NDVI Aur EVI Ka Funda`), aur time-lapse mein dikhata hai ki kaise ped kaat diye gaye. Ye koi chhoti baat nahi hai. Bazaar mein aisi courses 50,000 rupaye mein beche jaate hain aur bacchon ko sulaate hain. Tune yahan free mein, gully ke funde ke saath, asli code likhna seekha. Ab tu soch raha hoga, "Bhai, kya ho gaya life ka?" Dekh, tera kaam sirf code likhna nahi hai. Tera asli kaam hai — **Proof Dikhana**. Duniya bhar ki governments, NGOs, aur startups bol rahe hain "We are committed to stopping deforestation." BC sab bakwaas kar rahe hain. 90% ke paas data nahi hai, 10% ke paas data hai par unko samajh nahi aata ki kya karein. Tu wo 0.1% banda hai jiske paas asli tool hai. Tu satellite se data nikaal sakta hai, process kar sakta hai, aur ek aisa map bana sakta hai jo unke muh band kar de. Agar tu ye skill leke kisi climate-tech startup mein gaya, toh seedha CTO ban jaayega. Agar tu ye government ko dikhaya, toh policy change karwa sakta hai. Ye code sirf pixels nahi badal raha, ye zameen badal raha hai. > ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Abhi chapter padh ke soch mat lena "Bhai ho gaya, main expert ban gaya." Saale, ek project bana. Apne city ke aas-paas koi jungle ya green area chun. Pichle 5 saal ka Landsat ya Sentinel-2 data nikaal. NDVI calculate kar. Folium pe daal. Aur usko apne LinkedIn pe ya Twitter pe daal de. Jab log tujhse puchenge "Ye tune kaise kiya?", tab tera asli safar shuru hoga. Tab tak tu sirf theory padh raha tha. Bhai, tera asli gully ka don banna ab is project ko live karne par depend karta hai. Code likhna seekh liya, ab usse zinda karna seekh. Jaa, bhai-jaan, aag laga de!

# Jungle Bachao: Python Se Satellite Tracking Ka Khel

**Generated:** 7/18/2026
**Words:** 16,275
**Model:** glm-5.2

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Module 1: Bhai Ka Setup: Python Aur Satellite Data Ka Connection](#module-1-bhai-ka-setup-python-aur-satellite-data-ka-connection)

3. [Module 3: Baadal Hatao, Asli Scene Dekho](#module-3-baadal-hatao-asli-scene-dekho)
4. [Module 4: Hara Bhara Scene: NDVI Aur EVI Ka Funda](#module-4-hara-bhara-scene-ndvi-aur-evi-ka-funda)

- [Glossary](#glossary)

---

## Introduction

Abe sun, ye introduction hai, dhyan se padh nahi toh baad mein royega. Ek minute le, phone side pe rakh, aur dhyan se sun. Ye koi "Hello World" print karwane wali chindi Python book nahi hai. Ye real-world ka jhanjhat hai. Earth ka kachra ho raha hai, jungle kat rahe hain, aur hum bhai log GitHub pe 'dark mode' ka code copy paste kar rahe hain. Bhai, duniya jal rahi hai, literally. Agar tujhe apne code se kuch actually change karna hai, ya sachi mein 'Data Scientist' banne ka sapna dekhna hai, toh ghoomne ki jagah yahi baith aur ye book shuru se end tak khatam kar.

### Welcome and Purpose

Dekh bhai, sach sach bata. Tu intermediate Python developer hai. Tujhe loops, functions, pandas, aur thodi bhot machine learning bhi aati hai. Par jab baat real-world data ki aati hai, toh tu wahi atak jaata hai jahan sab atakte hain — "Bhai, data kahan se laun? Kaggle ke purane CSV se toh maza nahi aa raha." 

Is book ka purpose tujhe chhichlapan se nikal kar asli ground level par lana hai. Hum log satellite images process karenge. Aasman se dekh kar jo data aata hai, usko Python ke engine mein daal kar hum deforestation (jungle katne) ka hisaab lenge. Ye book tujhe ek chhote se Jupyter notebook ke andar qaid nahi rakhegi. Humara target hai ki tu ek aisa automated system banaye jo bina kisi kehta-e hue, aasman se aaye pixels ko padhe, samjhe, aur jab koi mamu ped katega, toh seedha alert bajaye. 

### What Readers Will Learn

Tujhe soch bhi nahi hoga ki open-source tools itne powerful ho sakte hain. Is book ke end tak, tu ek literal geo-spatial badass ban chuka hoga. Ye raha tujhe jo jo sikhne ko milega:

1. **Satellite Data Ka Jugaad:** Tujhe seekhayenge ki Google Earth Engine, Sentinel-2, ya Landsat jaise satellites se free mein high-quality data kaise khichta hai. Koi paisa nahi dena, bas thoda dimaag lagana hai.
2. **Raster Processing Ka Kamaal:** Rasterio aur Numpy ka combo tujhe aisa sikhayega ki tu ek 100MB ke tif file ko ek snap mein chota kar sake, uske pixels ko array mein badal ke math ka jaadu kar sake.
3. **Cloud Masking:** Satellite se aayi image mein agar baadal hain toh kya fayda? Tujhe seekhayenge baadlon ko code se "hatao, asli scene dikho" wala funda.
4. **Vegetation Indices:** NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) aur EVI ka asli funda. Hara bhara kya hai, sookha kya hai, ye pixels se pata chalega.
5. **Change Detection:** Do alag time ki images le, unko minus kar, aur bata ki bhai, itne number ke ped gayab ho gaye. Math itni simple aur itni deadly hai.
6. **Automation:** Bina chai peeye, script ko chalate rakhna. Jab tu so raha hai, tab script jagi rahe aur koi ghatna ho toh alert de.

Ab agar tu soch raha hai ki ye saara data apne 8GB RAM wale laptop pe download karega, toh tera laptop jal ke raakh ho jaayega aur teri maa kaate peet ke bahar nikal degi. GEE ka apna online code editor bhi hai (Code Editor), par wo JavaScript pe based hai. Aur tu Python ka raja hai. Toh hum Python ke `ee` (Earth Engine) library ka use karenge.

Lekin ye simple `pip install` se kaam nahi banega. Tu kisi bhi anjaan aadmi ko seedha apne ghar mein thodi ghusa lega? Google ko bhi security chahiye. Iske liye do cheezein chahiye:

### 3. Sentinel-2 Aur Landsat: Asli Hero Kaun?

Satellite imagery ka market mein bheed hai. Par jungle track karne ke liye do maamle hain: Resolution (kitna clear dikhe) aur Frequency (kitni din mein baad aaye).

- **Landsat (NASA ka satellite):** Ye hai local train ka general dabba. 30-meter resolution deta hai. Matlab ek pixel 30x30 meter ka area cover karega. Har 16 din mein ek baar aata hai. Free hai, purana data hai (1980s se). Agar tujhe bada area dekhna hai aur history check karni hai, toh Landsat apna bhai hai.
- **Sentinel-2 (ESA ka satellite):** Ye hai AC first class. 10-meter resolution deta hai. Ek pixel 10x10 meter ka area. Aur har 5 din mein data aata hai. Jungle kaatne wale ko pakadne ke liye ye best hai, kyunki 10 meter pe tu clear patches dekh sakta hai aur 5 din ka gap kaafi hota hai naye changes track karne ke liye.

Is chapter mein hum Sentinel-2 ko hi apna target banayenge, kyunki future mein jab hum NDVI (jungle ki health) nikalenge, toh Sentinel-2 ka 10-meter resolution humein razor-sharp results dega.

### Step 4: Sentinel-2 Ka Data Uthao (Apna Pehla Order)

Ab hum test karte hain ki system kaam kar raha hai ya nahi. Hum ek specific jagah ka Sentinel-2 data nikalenge. Maan le tu apne gaon ke paas ek jungle track karna chahta hai. Coordinates maan le `77.5, 10.0` (koi bhi location de sakta hai).

Dekh is code ko dhyan se. Ye asli scene hai.

```python
import ee

# Initialize the API
ee.Initialize()

# Apna target area define karo (Point geometry)
# Format: Longitude, Latitude
target_point = ee.Geometry.Point([77.5, 10.0])

# Sentinel-2 ka data collection load karo
# 'COPERNICUS/S2_HARMONIZED' is the standard dataset ID
s2_collection = ee.ImageCollection('COPERNICUS/S2_HARMONIZED') \
    .filterBounds(target_point) \
    .filterDate('2023-01-01', '2023-12-31') \
    .filter(ee.Filter.lt('CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE', 10))

# Collection mein kitne images hain?
count = s2_collection.size().getInfo()
print(f"Bhai, is area mein 2023 ke cloud-free images mil gaye: {count}")
```

**Kya hua upar?** 
- `ee.Geometry.Point` se humne Google ko bataya ki kahan ka data chahiye.
- `ee.ImageCollection` se humne Sentinel-2 ka poora database pakda.
- `.filterBounds` se sirf us point wale data filter kiye.
- `.filterDate` se saal 2023 ka data liya.
- `CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE < 10` isliye daala, kyunki satellite image mein agar baadal hai, toh tu jungle kya dekhega, sirf baadal dekhega. Baadal hatane ka proper tareeka hum Module 3 mein sikhenge, abhi bas basic filter.

Agar ye script chal gaya aur tujhe count mil gaya (jaise 20 ya 30 images), toh tera setup 100% perfect hai!

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Saale log `ee.ImageCollection` ki jagah `ee.Image` use kar dete hain. `Image` ek single photo hai. `ImageCollection` ek poora album hai. Satellite time-series ke liye hamesha `ImageCollection` use hota hai. Yaad rakh, warna error dega aur tu roya chale jaayega.

### Ek Sachchi Baat: Rate Limits Aur Patience

Bhai, tu free data use kar raha hai. Google bhi apne server pe load control mein rakhna chahta hai. Agar tu ek second mein 1000 requests bhejega, toh Google tujhe `Error: User rate limit exceeded` dega aur tera IP block kar dega thodi der ke liye.

Sentinel-2 5 din mein update hota hai. Agar tu 1 ghante ke andar alert chahta hai, toh satellite wrong choice hai, drone lagana padega. Satellite global scale ke liye hai, hyper-local ke liye nahi.

# Module 2: Rasterio Aur Numpy Ki Entrybaazi

Abe saale, Chapter 1 mein tune Earth Engine ka account banaya, authentication kiya, Python setup kiya. Bhai tera tool box toh ready hai, par abhi tak tune ek bhi satellite image ko khola nahi, chhua nahi. Ye aise hai ki tune Lamborghini kharidi, key utha ke pocket mein rakh li, par gaadi chalayi nahi. Aaj hum andar ghusenge. Satellite image ke actual pixels ko todenge. Hum Earth Engine ke cloud se bahar aakar local street pe aayenge. Yahan Rasterio aur Numpy raja hai. Ye dono bhai saale aasman se gir ke direct tere Python environment mein ghusenge. 

Dekh bhai, satellite image ek normal JPG ya PNG nahi hoti. Teri WhatsApp wali profile pic mein sirf 3 cheezein hoti hain — Red, Green, Blue (RGB). Par satellite image mein ye RGB ke saath saath Infrared, Thermal, aur kya kya nahi hota. Iske neeche geography chhupi hoti hai — kaunsa pixel Earth pe kahan hai? Ye sab handle karta hai **GeoTIFF** format. Aur is GeoTIFF ko todna, phaadna, aur pixel ka post-mortem karna seekhna hai aaj ka agenda. 

Agar ye chapter skip kiya, toh baad mein cloud masking aur change detection mein aisa phasoge ki Sharma ji ka beta bhi pity feel karega. Dhyan se padh, warna dimaag ka server hang ho jaayega.

## Asli Funda (Core Concepts)

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Awaam sochti hai satellite image ko `PIL` (Pillow) ya standard image viewer se khol lenge. BC usne image toh khul jaayegi, par uska spatial data (coordinates, projection) gaayab ho jaayega. Wo ek normal photo ban jaayegi. GeoTIFF sirf image nahi, wo data hai. Uski izzat kar.

### Rasterio: Tere Raster Ka Bouncer

Bhai, GeoTIFF file ek bhaari banduk hai. Usko directly Python mein ghusane ki koshish mat kar. Tujhe ek bouncer chahiye jo file ko safely read kare, metadata nikale, aur tere Python ke table pe serve kare. Wo bouncer hai **Rasterio**.

Rasterio ek Python library hai jo GDAL (Geospatial Data Abstraction Library) ko use karti hai. Par GDAL direct use karna is like trying to make khichdi in a pressure cooker without a whistle — explosion guaranteed. Rasterio usko simple banata hai.

Jab tu Rasterio se koi file kholt hai, toh wo tujhe do main cheezein deta hai:
1. **Metadata:** Image ka background check — resolution kya hai, CRS (Coordinate Reference System) kya hai, kitne bands hain.
2. **Numpy Array:** Actual pixel values. Ye wo numbers hain jiske saath hum tamasha karenge.

### Numpy: Asli Khiladi

Agar Rasterio bouncer hai, toh **Numpy** andar baith ke maalik hai. Satellite image ka har ek pixel ek number hota hai. Jaise infrared band mein ek pixel ki value 0 se 10000 ke beech ho sakti hai. Numpy ye saare numbers ko ek array (matrix) mein store karta hai. 

Bhai ye array aise samajh, jaise Mumbai ki local train ka dabba. Ek dabbe mein hazaro log (pixels) khade hain. Numpy tujhe power deta hai ki tu ek command mein poore dabbe ke logon ko ek sath bahar nikal de, ya sirf pehle wale 100 logon ko slice karke nikale. Multi-band image matlab 3-4 dabbe ek sath jud ke aaye hain. Hum inko slice karna seekhenge.

### CRS aur Projections: Map Ki Aukaat

Ab ye sabse important hissa hai. Sun meri baat dhyan se. 

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Har satellite image ke paas ek CRS hota hai. CRS bata raha hai ki "Bhai, main jo pixel tujhe de raha hu, wo Earth pe exactly kahan hai?" Agar CRS nahi hoga, toh wo pixel sirf ek screen pe kaala dabba hai, zameen pe koi aukaat nahi uski.

Earth gol hai (globe), par teri laptop screen flat hai. Gol cheez ko flat screen pe dikhane ke liye hum use 2D mein kheenchte hain. Is kheenchne ko projection kehte hain. Aur is kheenchne ka formula, usko CRS kehte hain.

Soch, ek orange ka chhilka utaar ke flat table pe bichha. Chhilka kahin toh phat jaayega, kahin khich jaayega. Ye distortion hota hai. Isliye duniya ke engineers ne alag-alag CRS bana diye. 
- **EPSG:4326:** Ye default hai. Latitude aur Longitude. Isme Earth ko degree mein napta hai. Google Maps isko use karta hai.
- **UTM Zones (jaise EPSG:32644):** Ye meters mein napta hai. Isme distortion kam hota hai area ke hisaab se. Sentinel-2 aur Landsat ka data aksar UTM mein aata hai.

Agar tu CRS bhool gaya, aur tu map pe ek pixel daal diya... BC Mumbai ka jungle dikha dega tu Shanghai mein. Projection galat hui toh tera deforestation hotspot 5 kilometer door chala jaayega. Saale, forest bachane gaya tha, kisi ki zameen ka kabza kar baithega.

## Practical Scene (Street-level Application)

Ab bahut bakwaas ho gayi. Chai thandi ho rahi hai. Code pe aate hain. 

Maan le tere paas ek GeoTIFF file hai — `sentinel2_mumbai.tif`. Ye file Mumbai ka satellite scene hai. Isme 4 bands hain: Blue, Green, Red, NIR (Near Infrared). 

### Step 1: File Khol Aur Peek Kar

Pehle Rasterio ko import kar. File ko `r` (read) mode mein khol. `with` statement use kar taaki kaam hone ke baad file apne aap band ho jaaye, warna memory leak hoga aur tera VS Code rone lagega.

```python
import rasterio
import numpy as np

# File ka path
file_path = 'sentinel2_mumbai.tif'

# Bouncer ko bulao
with rasterio.open(file_path) as src:
    # Pehle iska background check kar (Metadata)
    print(f"Kitne bands hain? {src.count}")
    print(f"Image ka size: {src.width} x {src.height} pixels")
    print(f"CRS kya hai bhai? {src.crs}")
    
    # Asli data nikal (Numpy array)
    # src.read() array deta hai shape mein (bands, rows, columns)
    image_array = src.read()
    
print("Array ka shape:", image_array.shape)
```

Dekh kya hua? `src.read()` ne poore data ko ek Numpy array mein daal diya. Agar shape `(4, 1000, 1000)` aaya, iska matlab 4 bands hain, aur har band 1000x1000 pixels ka hai. 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Rasterio arrays ko `(Bands, Rows, Columns)` format mein deta hai. Par OpenCV ya Matplotlib `(Rows, Columns, Bands)` maangega. Agar image plot karte time error aaye ki "dimensions don't match", toh samajh le tera array ulta pada hai. `np.transpose(image_array, (1, 2, 0))` use karke usko seedha kar lena.

### Step 2: Multi-Band Array Ko Slice Kar

Ab Numpy ka asli khel shuru. Maan le tujhe sirf Red aur NIR (Near Infrared) band chahiye, kyunki aage chal ke humko NDVI (vegetation index) nikalna hai. Pura array kyun load karna?

```python
with rasterio.open(file_path) as src:
    # Maan le Red 3rd band hai (index 2) aur NIR 4th band hai (index 3)
    # Rasterio mein 1-based indexing hoti hai, par Numpy 0-based khelta hai
    red_band = src.read(3) 
    nir_band = src.read(4)

print("Red band shape:", red_band.shape)
```

Saala dekh kitna easy tha! `src.read(3)` ne direct 3rd band ko nikaal ke de diya. Ab tere paas 2 alag alag arrays hain. Ek red light ka, ek infrared ka.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Indexing ka chakkar. Rasterio `read(1)` se shuru hota hai (1-based). Par Numpy array `image_array[0]` se shuru hota hai (0-based). Confuse hua toh galat band nikal lega. Red ki jagah Blue nikal lega, aur tu sochega jungle sookh gaya, BC satellite ne blue filter lagaya tha!

### Step 3: Array Ka Post-Mortem (Numpy Operations)

Ab hum in pixels ko manipulate karenge. Satellite data mein kabhi kabhi garbage values aati hain. Jaise cloud cover ya sensor error ki wajah se pixel ki value `0` ya `-9999` aa jaati hai. Inko handle karna padta hai.

```python
# Red band mein agar koi -9999 (NoData) value hai, toh usko 0 kar do
# Numpy ki masking power use karke
red_band_clean = np.where(red_band == -9999, 0, red_band)

# Dekh bhai, satellite mein pixel values reflectance ki hoti hain. 
# Kabhi kabhi 0 se 10000 tak jaati hain.
# Hum check karenge ki maximum aur minimum kya hai
print(f"Red band min value: {red_band_clean.min()}")
print(f"Red band max value: {red_band_clean.max()}")
```

### 1. QA Bands: Satellite ki Quality Checking

Saale, satellite sirf photo nahi leta. Ye thoda bawal cheez hai. Sentinel-2 (ESA ka satellite) aur Landsat (NASA ka satellite) ke sensors hote hain. Ye log image ke sath-sath ek extra file ya extra layer bhi dete hain jisko hum bolte hain **QA Band** (Quality Assurance Band). 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** QA band ek tarah ka satellite ka "review" hai. Jaise restaurant mein khane ke baad manager poochta hai "How was the food?", waise hi satellite bolta hai "Bhai, is pixel ka scene clear hai, ya cloud hai, ya shadow hai?" QA band mein ek number hota hai, aur us number ko binary (0 aur 1) mein tod ke hum exactly pata laga lete hain ki is pixel mein kya bakwaas hai.

Jaise, Sentinel-2 ka `SCL` (Scene Classification Layer) aata hai. Isme numbers hote hain:
- 0: No Data (Khaali)
- 1: Saturated/Defective (Kharab pixel)
- 2: Dark Area Pixels (Andhera)
- 3: Cloud Shadows (Baadal ka saaya)
- 4: Vegetation (Jungle/Khet)
- 5: Not Vegetated (Bina ped wali zameen)
- 6: Water (Paani)
- 7: Unclassified (Pata nahi kya hai)
- 8: Cloud Medium Probability (Baadal aa raha hai)
- 9: Cloud High Probability (Pakka baadal)
- 10: Cirrus (Hawa mein upar wala baadal)

Dekh, tujhe kya karna hai? Tujhe 3, 8, 9, aur 10 number wale pixels ko apni image se nikalna hai. Bas. Itna easy hai ki tera pet ka kutta bhi samajh jaaye.

### 2. Masking Algorithms: Kachra Hata, Asli Data La

Ab Numpy Array ka use karenge. Pichle chapter mein humne `Numpy` aur `Rasterio` ki entrybaazi ki thi na? Wahi aaj kaam aayegi. 

Masking ka funda simple hai. Tere paas ek array hai image ka. Tere paas dusra array hai QA band ka. Tu QA band mein dekhta hai ki bhai jahan pixel value 8, 9 ya 10 hai, wahan cloud hai. Toh tu Numpy ka magic use karke bolta hai: "Bhai, main image ke array mein jahan cloud hai, wahan ki value `NaN` (Not a Number) ya 0 kar de." 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Abe saale, cloud pixels ko 0 mat bana. Agar tu 0 kar doga, toh tera model usko "perfect black surface" samajh lega (jaise raat ka andhera ya pahaad ka kaala patthar). Hamesha `NaN` use kar, kyunki `NaN` ka matlab hai "yahan data hi nahi hai". Numpy mein `np.nan` use kar. Samjha?

Ek chhota sa code snippet dekh, dimag lagaa:

```python
import rasterio
import numpy as np

# Image aur QA band kholo (maan le dono ek hi GeoTIFF mein hain ya alag)
with rasterio.open('sentinel2_image.tif') as src:
    image = src.read()
    qa_band = src.read(1) # Maan le pehla band QA band hai (sirf example)

# Cloud mask banao: SCL values 3, 8, 9, 10 ko dhoondo
cloud_mask = np.isin(qa_band, [3, 8, 9, 10])

# Mask True jahan clouds hain. Ab image mein wahan NaN daal do.
# image shape (bands, rows, cols) hota hai
image_masked = np.where(cloud_mask, np.nan, image)
```
Dekha? Itna simple tha. `np.isin` se mask ban gaya, `np.where` se usko image pe laga diya. Ab teri image mein jahan baadal tha, wahan `NaN` aa gaya. Asli scene!

### 3. Time-Series Composites: Jugaad For The Win

Ab mask toh laga diya. Par image mein 40% jagah `NaN` hai. Toh kya hua? Ab aayega asli street hustle. Time-Series Composite.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Ek din ka satellite image kabhi perfect nahi hota. Agar 1 Jan ko baadal tha, toh 6 Jan ko clear ho sakta hai. Toh hum 1 Jan se 31 Jan tak ki saari images download karte hain, un sab mein se cloud wale pixels hataate hain, aur baaki bache hue clear pixels ko ek sath jod (mosaic) karke ek "Composite Image" banate hain. Ye image itni clean hoti hai jaise arranged marriage ki ladki ke ghar wale photo bhejte hain — sab best angles, zero flaws.

Isko bolte hain **Median Compositing**. Hum ek mahine ke saare clear pixels nikalte hain, aur unka `median` (madhyam) nikaal lete hain. Median isliye kyunki agar koi pixel kisi din kharab aa gaya, toh median usko ignore kar deta hai. Mean (average) use mat karna, warna ek extreme value tera pura data latak jayega.

```python
# Maan le tere paas 5 images hain ek mahine ki (sab masked hain)
# stack shape: (5, bands, rows, cols)
stacked_images = np.array([img1, img2, img3, img4, img5]) 

# Median nikaalo axis=0 pe, kyunki 0th dimension images ka hai
composite_image = np.nanmedian(stacked_images, axis=0)
```
`np.nanmedian` use kiya, kyunki ye `NaN` values ko ignore karke median nikaalta hai. Bhai, ye line code ki nahi, jadu hai. Ekdum chamakdar, cloud-free image mil jayegi.

**Step 2: Cloud Mask Function likho GEE mein**
GEE mein bhi hum QA band use karke mask laga sakte hain. Sentinel-2 ke `SCL` band ka use karenge.

```python
def mask_s2_clouds(image):
    # SCL band uthao
    scl = image.select('SCL')
    # Cloud (8,9,10) aur Shadow (3) ki condition banao
    cloud_shadow_bitmask = 3
    cloud_bitmask = 8
    cirrus_bitmask = 9
    high_cloud = 10
    
    # 0 jahan clear hai, 1 jahan cloud/shadow hai
    mask = scl.neq(cloud_shadow_bitmask).And(
           scl.neq(cloud_bitmask)).And(
           scl.neq(cirrus_bitmask)).And(
           scl.neq(high_cloud))
    
    # Mask apply karke image return karo
    return image.updateMask(mask)
```
Ye function GEE pe server side chalega. Bhai, GEE ka server itna powerful hai, tu isko 100 saal ka data bhi doge, ye 2 second mein mask laga dega. Ye cloud computing ka jugaad hai. Apne PC pe download karke Numpy pe math lagaane se bach gaya!

**Step 3: Composite Banao**
Ab is masked collection ka median composite banao.

```python
# Collection mein har image pe mask function apply karo, phir median lo
composite = s2_collection.map(mask_s2_clouds).median()

# Ab is composite ko apne local PC pe download karo (Data Pull)
# Ya GEE pe hi NDVI calculate kar (jo hum agle chapter mein karenge)
```

## Asli Funda (Core Concepts)

### 1. NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) - Jung ki Aadharshila

Dekh bhai, asli scene ye hai ki har ped, ghaas, ya patta ekdum specific tarike se sunlight ko absorb (peeka) aur reflect (wapas throw) karta hai. 

Jab dhoop ped pe padti hai, toh ped ke andar ke chlorophyll (wo hi jo patta ko hara banata hai) Visible Light ka 'Red' band ekdum faad ke peek leta hai. Kyunki usko wo light photosynthesis ke liye chahiye. Lekin ekdum uske opposite, 'Near-Infrared' (NIR) light ko wo absorb nahi karta, balki usko ekdum zordar reflect kar deta hai. Jaise autorickshaw wala minimum fare wale passenger ko baithne se rok deta hai.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Healthy plant = Red light ki absorption high, NIR light ka reflection high. Dead plant ya barren zameen = Dono ko almost barabar reflect karega. Ye distinction hi humara asli jadoo hai.

Ab isko math mein convert karte hain. Kyunki hum tapori log bhi formula ka respect karte hain. Formula hai:
**NDVI = (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)**

Samjha? Nahi? Chal, biryani ke example se samajh. 
Maan le tu biryani kha raha hai. NIR tere khaaye hue biryani ke massale hain, aur Red tere plate mein bache hue pyaz hain. Agar biryani ekdum mast hai (healthy vegetation), toh massala zyada hoga aur pyaz kam (NIR high, Red low). NDVI nikalne ka formula bas ye check karta hai ki teri plate mein massale aur pyaz ka ratio kya hai. 'Normalized' isliye kyunki ye -1 se +1 ke beech mein value deta hai, chahe satellite ka sensor kitna bhi advanced ho. 

**Threshold Values (Ye values yaad rakh, dimag mein chipka le):**
- **-1 se 0 tak:** Ye paani hai, baraf hai, ya road ka kachra. Yahan jungle nahi hai. (NIR < Red)
- **0 se 0.2 tak:** Barren zameen, tooti hui mitti, ya ghaas jo sookh chuki hai. Sharma ji ka genda yahan charne aata hai.
- **0.2 se 0.5 tak:** Thin vegetation. Kekar ped, jhaadiyaan, ya thoda healthy ghaas.
- **0.6 se 0.8+ tak:** Dense, ekdum kadak forest. Amazon, Sundarbans, ya Western Ghats. Ekdum taaza, hara-bhara jungle.

Agar tujhe deforestation pakdni hai, toh tujhe exactly pata hona chahiye ki tera area of interest kabhi 0.7 pe tha, aur ab 0.1 pe kyu aa gaya hai. 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Saale, kabhi mat sochna ki NDVI > 0.6 ka matlab hamesha dense forest hi hota hai. Kabhi kabhi khet (agriculture) ke ped bhi peak season mein 0.8+ NDVI de dete hain. Apna area map pe dekh, context samajh, phir label lagaa. Blindly code chalake mat baith.

### 2. EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index) - NDVI Ka Bada Bhai

Ab tu soch raha hoga, "Bhai, jab NDVI itna mast hai, toh EVI kyu chahiye?" 
Dekh, NDVI ka ek bada problem hai. Jaise monsoon mein Mumbai ka traffic jam hota hai, waise hi jab jungle bahut ghana ho (dense canopy), toh NDVI saturate ho jata hai. Matlab NIR aur Red dono itna high reflect karenge ki formula ki value 0.8 ke aas-paas atak jayegi. Tu ye nahi bata paayega ki ped 1 meter lamba hai ya 50 meter lamba. Dono same dikhega. 

Aur dusra problem? Atmospheric aerosols. Dhool, dhuaan, ya halka baadal (jo **Baadal Hatao** mein bach gaya) NDVI ko bigad deta hai. Yahan entry hoti hai EVI ki. 

EVI ekdum premium hai. Ye atmospheric correction aur background soil brightness ko adjust karta hai. Iske liye ye 'Blue' band ka bhi use karta hai. 
**EVI = G * ((NIR - Red) / (NIR + C1 * Red - C2 * Blue + L))**

G, C1, C2, L ye sab coefficients hain (G=2.5, C1=6, C2=7.5, L=1). NASA ne already inko tune kar rakha hai. Tujhe bas formula code mein utarna hai.

EVI ki value normally 0 se 1 ke beech hoti hai (kabhi -1 tak bhi ja sakta hai par mostly 0 to 1). Dense forest ke liye EVI 0.4 se 0.8 ka area maanta hai. EVI tujhe wahan clear difference dikhayega jahan NDVI "sab mast hai" bolke so jayega.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Agar tujhe kisi aise jungle ka track karna hai jo bahut ghana hai (jaise Northeast India ya Amazon), toh NDVI mat use karna, warna tu subtle changes miss karega. EVI daal apne code mein. NDVI simple areas ke liye best hai, EVI pro-level analysis ke liye.

### Step 1: NDVI Ka Jugaad

```python
import rasterio
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Bhai, file paths apne system ke hisaab se daal
# Maan le rahe hain tere paas ek clipped GeoTIFF hai jisme 3 bands hain:
# Band 1: Blue, Band 2: Red, Band 3: NIR (Sentinel-2 ke standard hisaab se nahi, bas example ke liye)
file_path = 'clipped_forest_scene.tif'

# Rasterio se file khol aur read kar
with rasterio.open(file_path) as src:
    # Agar bands alag alag files hain toh src.read(1) etc use karna
    # Yahan maan rahe hain single file mein bands hain
    blue = src.read(1).astype('float32')
    red = src.read(2).astype('float32')
    nir = src.read(3).astype('float32')
    profile = src.profile # Metadata save kar rakh, baad mein kaam aayega

# Numpy division mein zero se divide hote hi error/dimag kharab hota hai
# Isliye ek chota sa trick lagate hain. NIR aur Red ka sum zero nahi hona chahiye
# Numpy mein 'where' use karke safe division kar
np.seterr(divide='ignore', invalid='ignore')

# NDVI Formula: (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)
ndvi = np.empty_like(nir) # Khali array bana
ndvi = (nir - red) / (nir + red)

# NaN (Not a Number) values ko 0 ya -1 se replace kar, warna map pe gadha dikhega
ndvi = np.nan_to_num(ndvi, nan=-1.0)

print(f"NDVI Stats - Min: {ndvi.min()}, Max: {ndvi.max()}")
```

Dekh code kaisa chota aur saaf hai? Saale log complex library dhundte hain, BC formula itna simple hai ki tera pet ka kutta bhi samajh jaaye. Bas array operations ka khel hai.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** Numpy arrays ko `float32` mein convert karna zaroori hai. Kyunki satellite data aksar integer (0-10000 ya 0-65535) mein aata hai. Agar tu integer mein hi divide karega, toh 3/5 = 0 dega, 4/5 = 0 dega. Tera pura jungle barren dikhega. Dimaag mat kha, float mein convert kar phir calculate kar.

### Step 3: EVI Ka Code (Pro Level)

Ab NDVI ka bada bhai EVI ko bhi code karte hain. Thoda formula lamba hai, par darna mat. 

```python
# EVI Formula: G * ((NIR - Red) / (NIR + C1*Red - C2*Blue + L))
# Constants: G=2.5, C1=6, C2=7.5, L=1
G = 2.5
C1 = 6.0
C2 = 7.5
L = 1.0

# EVI calculation
evi_numerator = G * (nir - red)
evi_denominator = (nir + C1 * red - C2 * blue + L)

# Phir se zero division ka dhyan rakho
evi = np.empty_like(nir)
evi = np.divide(evi_numerator, evi_denominator, out=np.zeros_like(evi_numerator), where=evi_denominator!=0)

# Values ko -1 se 1 ke beech clamp kar lete hain, warna kuch weird values aa jati hain
evi = np.clip(evi, -1.0, 1.0)

print(f"EVI Stats - Min: {evi.min()}, Max: {evi.max()}")

# EVI ka map bhi dekh le
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 10))
plt.imshow(evi, cmap='YlGn', vmin=0, vmax=0.8) # Yellow to Green
plt.colorbar(label='EVI Values')
plt.title('Dense Forest Analysis (EVI)')
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()
```

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** EVI mein kabhi kabhi blue band ki wajah se denominator zero ya negative ho jata hai, aur tere map pe random bright white/black dots (noise) aa jate hain. `np.clip()` use karke values ko strictly -1 se 1 ke beech bandh de. Ye tere map ki dignity bachayega.

### Step 4: Threshold Laga Ke Deforestation Demarcate Karna

Ab aata hai asli game. Map dekh ke toh sab karte hain. Par automation kaise hogi? Computer ko kaise batayega ki "bhai ye area forest nahi raha"? 

Yahan thresholding ka funda aata hai. Hum set karenge ki agar NDVI 0.4 se zyada hai, toh wo forest hai. Agar kam hai, toh wo deforested ya barren hai.

```python
# Maan le humara rule hai: NDVI > 0.4 = Healthy Forest
# NDVI <= 0.4 = Deforested / Barren / Water

# Numpy mein boolean masking ka use karke ye karte hain
# 'forest_mask' ek array hoga jahan True hoga jahan jungle hai, False jahan nahi hai
forest_mask = ndvi > 0.4

# Is mask ko integer mein badal (True=1, False=0) taaki save kar saken
forest_mask_int = forest_mask.astype(np.uint8)

# Ab is mask ko naya GeoTIFF bana ke save kar lete hain
# Ye tere alert system (next chapter) mein bahut kaam aayega
out_profile = profile.copy()
out_profile.update(
    dtype=rasterio.uint8,
    count=1,
    nodata=0 # 0 matlab no forest, 1 matlab forest
)

with rasterio.open('forest_mask.tif', 'w', **out_profile) as dst:
    dst.write(forest_mask_int, 1)

print("Forest mask map ban gaya bhai! 'forest_mask.tif' check kar.")
```

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Threshold value (jaise 0.4) kabhi bhi permanently apne code mein hardcode mat chhod. Har region ka threshold alag hota hai. Rajasthan ke jungle ka threshold Kerala ke jungle se alag hoga. Apne study area ke liye NDVI ka histogram plot karke dekh, phir threshold decide kar. Ekdum blind 0.4 mat le.

## Asli Funda (Core Concepts)

### 1. Image Differencing: Bhai, Minus Wala Khel
Sabse pehla basic sawaal: Do images mein difference kaise pata karein? Bhai, primary school mein maths mein kya padha tha? Minus! 

Jaise tu biryani order karta hai, aur agar delivery aane pe tumhe pehle se pata hai ki 3 tukde khaane ke baad 1 bacha hai, toh matlab 2 tukde khatam ho gaye. Waise hi, hum NDVI (ya koi bhi index) ki do images lenge—ek purani (Time 1) aur ek nayi (Time 2). 

Numpy Array mein dono ko ghusa, aur bas ek minus kar de:
`Change = NDVI_Time2 - NDVI_Time1`

Agar kisi jagah pehle NDVI 0.8 tha (dense jungle) aur ab 0.2 hai (barren zameen), toh difference -0.6 hoga. Negative value ka matlab? Ped gayab! Yeh negative value hi tera pehla clue hai ki yahan gaddari hui hai. 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Saale, date aur time mat bhool. Agar tu January (winter) aur June (summer) ki image compare karega, toh jungle naturally patla lagega. Tu chup-chap khud ko dhokha de dega aur bolega "Deforestation ho gaya!" Nahi hua BC, seasonal change hua. Same season, same month, alag saal ki image use kar. Samjha? Nahi samjha? Padh dubara, dimag lagaa.

### 2. Thresholding: Kahan Se Lakir Khinchu?
Theek hai, tune minus kar diya. Ab array mein hazaron pixels hain. Kuch mein -0.6 hai, kuch mein +0.4 hai (naya ped ugg gaya), aur kuch mein 0.01 hai (kuch nahi badla, bas sensor ka nautanki). Ab tu kaise decide karega ki kahan actual ped kaate gaye?

Yahan aata hai **Thresholding** ka funda. Ye aise samajh, jaise local train mein general compartment mein entry karne ka rule. Agar 5 se zyada log andar hain, toh "Bheed hai". Agar 5 ya kam hain, toh "Jagah hai". 

Hum ek cutoff value set karenge. Jaise:
`Deforestation = Change < -0.3`

Matlab, agar NDVI ka drop 0.3 se zyada hai, tabhi hum manenge ki ped kaata gaya. Yeh threshold magical nahi hota bhai. Tu 0.2 bhi rakh sakta hai, 0.4 bhi. Iske liye thoda data dekh ke hit-and-trial karna padta hai. Ekdum arranged marriage ki tarah—pehle dekh lena thoda, phir commitment karna.

### 3. Raster se Vector: Polygon Nikalo 
Ab tak jo output mila, wo Numpy Array mein hai. Yaani GeoTIFF ke pixels. Ek pixel kitna bada hota hai Sentinel-2 mein? 10 meter x 10 meter. Ab tu collector ko bolega, "Bhai, pixel number 4523 aur 4524 pe deforestation hai"? Collector kya, khud bhi samajh nahi aayega.

Humein in pixels ko group karna hoga aur ekdum clean **Vector Polygons** banana hain. Jaise map pe ek plot ka boundary draw hota hai na? Waise hi. Iske liye hum use karenge ek badass library ka: `rasterio.features.shapes`. Ye array ke contiguous (judwe) pixels ko utha ke unka polygon bana deta hai. Fir hum in polygons ko GeoJSON ya Shapefile mein save kar sakte hain.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Jab tu polygons bana raha hai, toh area filter lagana padta hai. Ek akele ped (10x10 meter) ka polygon banake kya faayda? Ek kutte ko susu karte time ped pe lag gayi, wo bhi change dikhega. Minimum area threshold rakh, jaise 0.5 hectare (5,000 sq meters) se kam wale polygons ko seedha reject kar de.

### 4. False Positives: Jugaad Aur Filter
Gully ka sabse bada rule: Kisi pe bhi bharosa mat karna. Satellite data bhi same hai. Tune threshold laga diya, bahut saare polygons aa gaye. Ab kya sab sach hai? Bilkul nahi!

False positives ka matlab, jahan change nahi hua, but system bole "Hua hai". Ye kyun hota hai?
1. **Clouds ka nautanki:** Tune "Baadal Hatao, Asli Scene Dekho" wala chapter skip kiya kya? Agar Time 1 mein ek chhota sa baadal tha jo mask nahi hua, toh NDVI wahan -0.5 ka drop dikhayega. System bolega ped kaat diya, actually sirf baadal tha.
2. **Shadows:** Pahaad ki chhaon ya building ki chhaon bhi NDVI girati hai.
3. **Water level fluctuation:** Gaon ka taal (pond) sookh gaya, toh NDVI girega, but wo deforestation nahi hai.

Isliye bhai, accuracy badhane ke liye tujhe filter lagana hi padega. QA Band aur Median Compositing ka use karke data clean rakh, aur jo polygons aaye hain unme se unko hata de jinka area bahut chhota hai ya shape bahut weird hai (jaise ek lambi patli line, jo shayad nala ho ya road ho).

## Practical Scene (Street-level Application)

Chalo, gyaan peene ke bajaye ab code likhte hain. Yahan hum Sentinel-2 ka data lenge. Sentinel-2 (ESA ka satellite) best hai kyunki iska 10-meter resolution dete hai, aur 5 din mein update hota hai. Hum 2022 aur 2023 ka same month ka data lenge.

Pehle libraries import kar:
```python
import ee
import rasterio
from rasterio.features import shapes
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import shape
```

### Step 1: GEE Authentication aur Data Pull
Bhai, agar takkar ki image chahiye toh GEE (Google Earth Engine) kaam aata hai. Apna Earth Engine Account: aur Authentication: setup kar le. Hum ek specific region (jaise Amazon ka ek plot) ka ROI (Region of Interest) lenge.

```python
ee.Initialize()

# Region of Interest define kar (koi bhi point le le)
roi = ee.Geometry.Point([-62.2, -3.5]).buffer(10000).bounds()

# Function to get NDVI image for a specific date range
def get_ndvi(start_date, end_date):
    # Sentinel-2 data pull
    collection = ee.ImageCollection('COPERNICUS/S2_HARMONIZED') \
        .filterBounds(roi) \
        .filterDate(start_date, end_date) \
        .filter(ee.Filter.lt('CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE', 10)) \
        .map(lambda img: img.normalizedDifference(['B8', 'B4']).rename('NDVI'))
    
    # Median compositing to remove residual clouds
    return collection.median().clip(roi)

# Time 1: Purana data (2022)
img_2022 = get_ndvi('2022-07-01', '2022-08-01')

# Time 2: Naya data (2023)
img_2023 = get_ndvi('2023-07-01', '2023-08-01')
```

Dekh, yahan humne `CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE` 10% rakha. Baadalon ke chakkar mein mat phasna. Median compositing bhi kiya, taaki ekdum smooth image mile.

### Step 2: Local Download aur Numpy Array Mein Convert
GEE pe compute kar sakte hain, lekin asli street control tab aata hai jab data apne Python Setup: mein ho. GeoTIFF download kar aur Rasterio se read kar.

```python
# GEE se download URL lo (maan le humne dono images download kar liye)
# Ab unhe Rasterio se read karte hain

with rasterio.open('ndvi_2022.tif') as src1:
    ndvi_2022 = src1.read(1).astype('float32')
    profile = src1.profile

with rasterio.open('ndvi_2023.tif') as src2:
    ndvi_2023 = src2.read(1).astype('float32')
```

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Dono images ka EPSG (projection) aur resolution bilkul same hona chahiye. Agar ek 10 meter ka hai aur dusra 20 meter ka, toh Numpy array ke sizes match nahi karenge aur minus karte time error dega. Isliye hamesha Metadata: check kar. EPSG:4326 ya UTM Zones (jaise EPSG:32644) dono mein same hona chahiye.

### Step 3: Image Differencing Aur Thresholding
Ab asli gully ka kaam. Dono arrays ko minus kar.

```python
# Calculate change
change = ndvi_2023 - ndvi_2022

# Thresholding: Deforestation ke liye drop > 0.3 hona chahiye
# Masking: Sirf wahi pixels rakh jo actually jungle the pehle (NDVI > 0.5)
deforestation_mask = (change < -0.3) & (ndvi_2022 > 0.5)

# Convert boolean to integer (1 for deforestation, 0 for nothing)
deforestation_array = deforestation_mask.astype('uint8')
```

Kya kiya humne? `change < -0.3` se bahut bada drop pakda. Aur `ndvi_2022 > 0.5` lagaya kyunki agar pehle se hi khaali plot tha (NDVI 0.1), toh usme drop nahi aayega. Sirf actual jungle ko target kiya. Samjha? Nahi? Thik hai, aage padh.

### Step 4: Raster to Vector Polygons
Ab array mein 1s aur 0s hain. 1s ko group karke polygon banana hai.

```python
# Rasterio.features.shapes use karke polygons banao
results = (
    {'properties': {'raster_val': v}, 'geometry': s}
    for i, (s, v) in enumerate(
        shapes(deforestation_array, transform=profile['transform'], connectivity=8))
    if v == 1  # Sirf deforestation wale pixels
)

# GeoDataFrame mein convert karo
geo_list = list(results)
gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame.from_features(geo_list, crs=profile['crs'])
```

Bhai, `connectivity=8` ka matlab hai ki 8 directions (upar, neeche, left, right, aur 4 diagonals) se judwe pixels ko ek polygon mana jayega. Ye standard practice hai.

### Step 5: False Positives Filter Karke Accuracy Badhao
Ab tere paas `gdf` naam ka GeoDataFrame hai. Isme bahut saare polygons honge. Kuch bade honge, kuch chote. Choto wale fake hain. Area calculate karke unko nikal de.

```python
# Area calculate kar (meter squared mein, agar UTM projection hai)
gdf['area_sqm'] = gdf.geometry.area

# Filter: Sirf wahi polygons rakh jinka area 5000 sq meters (0.5 hectare) se zyada ho
clean_gdf = gdf[gdf['area_sqm'] > 5000]

# Shapefile save kar
clean_gdf.to_file('deforestation_hotspots.shp')
```

Bas! Tere paas ek Shapefile hai jisme sirf asli deforestation wale polygons hain. Isko QGIS ya ArcGIS mein khol, ya agle chapter mein map pe chharenge. Ek baat dhyaan rakhna, agar polygons bahut zyada weird shape ke aa rahe hain, toh `gdf.geometry.simplify(tolerance=10)` use karke unhe smooth kar sakta hai. Jaise autorickshaw ka meter, thoda adjust kar lena.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** Bhai, 100% accuracy ka sapna mat dekh. Satellite data mein hamesha kuch noise hota hai. 80-90% accuracy pe kaam chal jaayega. Baaki 10% false positives ko manual check se nikalna padega. Asli zindagi mein bhi koi perfect nahi hota, na tu perfect code likhega. Aim tera yeh hona chahiye ki automated system tujhe 100 jagah alert de, aur tu 80 jagah pakka deforestation dhoondh le. Wo enough hai.

### Summary of the Street:
1. **Minus kar (Image Differencing):** Time 2 - Time 1. Negative matlab ped kat gaya.
2. **Lakir khinch (Thresholding):** Sirf drop > 0.3 wale pakdo. Chhote fluctuations ignore kar.
3. **Polygon bana (Vectorization):** Numpy array se GeoDataFrame bana. Rasterio.features.shapes ka use kar.
4. **Jugaad kar (Filtering):** Area < 0.5 hectare wale seedha dustbin mein daal de. False positives ko maaro goli.

Agar tune ye steps properly follow kiye, toh tera system ekdum tight ho gaya hai. Tu ab woh don hai jo satellite ki aankh se dekh raha hai ki zameen ke kaunse hisse mein gaddari ho rahi hai.

Samjha? Nahi? Padh dubara, dimag lagaa.

### 1. Timer Ka Kaam: Cron Jobs Ka Jugaad

Saale, tu Windows mein task scheduler use karta tha apne PC mein, but ab hum cloud pe khel rahe hain. Yahan **Cron Jobs** chalta hai. Cron ek time-based job scheduler hai Linux/Unix systems mein. 

Cron expression dikhta hai aise: `0 6 * * *`
Ye kya bolta hai? 
- Pehla `0`: Minute (0th minute)
- Doosra `6`: Hour (6 baje)
- Teesra `*`: Din (har din)
- Choutha `*`: Month (har mahine)
- Paanchwa `*`: Weekday (har hafta)

Toh `0 6 * * *` matlab "Bhai, har din subah 6 baje uth jaa aur kaam kar." 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Satellite data UTC time mein aata hai. India UTC+5:30 hai. Agar tujhe subah 10 baje Indian time pe data chahiye, toh UTC mein 4:30 AM set karna padega. Time zone ka chakkar na samajh ke mat ch*diya kar.

### 2. Karkhana: GEE Batch Jobs vs AWS Lambda

Ab data aaya, script run kahan hoga? Tere PC pe? Saale, agar bijli chali gayi toh? Agar tera wifi down hua toh? 

Isliye hum cloud use karte hain. Do main options hain:
- **GEE Batch Jobs:** Earth Engine mein tujhe `ee.batch.Export` ya cloud functions chalane ka option hai. Ye free mein milta hai (limits ke andar), aur Google ke servers pe chalta hai. Tera script GEE ke environment mein hi chalega.
- **AWS Lambda / Google Cloud Functions:** Ye serverless computing hai. Bhai, ye aise samajh, jaise tu autorickshaw rent pe leta hai sirf tab jab travel karna ho. Baaki time parking aur maintenance ka kharcha nahi dena. Tu sirf wahi paisa dega jab tera function chalega. 

Hum AWS Lambda use karenge kyunki wo real-world production standard hai. Ek zip file banayenge jisme Rasterio, Numpy, aur tera deforestation logic hoga, aur usko Lambda pe daal denge.

Email bhejna ab bohot boomer hai. Saale, startup wale sab Telegram pe chalte hain. Telegram bots free hain, instant hain, aur API itna easy hai ki tera pet ka kutta bhi message bhej dega.

```python
credentials = ee.ServiceAccountCredentials(service_account, 'path/to/private-key.json')

# Telegram Token aur Chat ID
```

### Step 2: Data Pull Aur Cloud Masking (Jaldi Se)

Bhai, humne Module 3 mein Baadal Hatao seekha tha. Wahi QA band wala logic yahan lagega. Lambda pe time aur memory limit hoti hai, isliye script tight rakh.

```python
def mask_s2_clouds(image):
    # Sentinel-2 ka QA band
    qa = image.select('QA60')
    # Bit 10 aur 11 clouds aur cirrus hain
    cloud_bit_mask = 1 << 10
    cirrus_bit_mask = 1 << 11
    mask = qa.bitwiseAnd(cloud_bit_mask).eq(0) and \
           qa.bitwiseAnd(cirrus_bit_mask).eq(0)
    return image.updateMask(mask).divide(10000)

# Apna Area of Interest (AOI) define kar
aoi = ee.Geometry.Rectangle([77.5, 12.9, 77.6, 13.0]) # Bangalore ke aas paas

# Aaj aur kal ki date nikal
today = ee.Date(datetime.now())
yesterday = today.advance(-1, 'day')

# Data pull
today_img = ee.ImageCollection("COPERNICUS/S2") \
    .filterBounds(aoi) \
    .filterDate(yesterday, today) \
    .map(mask_s2_clouds) \
    .median()

# Pichle hafte ka reference image lo
last_week = today.advance(-7, 'day')
ref_img = ee.ImageCollection("COPERNICUS/S2") \
    .filterBounds(aoi) \
    .filterDate(last_week, yesterday) \
    .map(mask_s2_clouds) \
    .median()
```

Dekh, `median()` compositing use kiya kyunki ek din mein multiple images aati hain aur median se baadal ka kachra clean ho jata hai. (Module 3 yaad rakh, BC).

### Step 3: NDVI aur Change Detection Ka Math

Ab in dono images se NDVI nikal. Bhai, NDVI ka formula toh tera pet ka kutta bhi jaanta hai ab: `(NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)`.

```python
def add_ndvi(image):
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(['B8', 'B4']).rename('NDVI')
    return image.addBands(ndvi)

today_ndvi = add_ndvi(today_img).select('NDVI')
ref_ndvi = add_ndvi(ref_img).select('NDVI')

# Change calculate karo
change = ref_ndvi.subtract(today_ndvi)

# Agar NDVI 0.2 se zyada gira hai, toh pakka ped kaat rahe hain
deforestation_mask = change.gt(0.2)

# Area calculate karna hai. GEE mein area calculator use karo
# Pixel area multiply karo mask ke count se
deforested_area = deforestation_mask.multiply(ee.Image.pixelArea()).reduceRegion(
    reducer=ee.Reducer.sum(),
    geometry=aoi,
    scale=30,
    maxPixels=1e9
).getInfo()

# Area square meters mein aayega, hectares mein convert kar
area_ha = deforested_area.get('NDVI', 0) / 10000
```

Samjha? Humne `gt(0.2)` lagaya. Agar NDVI ka drop 0.2 se zyada hai, matlab pehle jungle tha, ab concrete ya kachra ban gaya. Phir `pixelArea()` se uska area nikaal liya.

```python
def send_telegram_message(message):
    url = f"https://api.telegram.org/bot{TELEGRAM_TOKEN}/sendMessage"
    payload = {
        "chat_id": CHAT_ID,
        "text": message,
        "parse_mode": "Markdown"
    }
    response = requests.post(url, json=payload)
    return response.json()

# Logic check
if area_ha > 5.0:
    msg = f"🚨 *ALERT: PED KAAT RAHE HAIN!*\n\n" \
          f"*Area:* Bangalore Outskirts\n" \

    send_telegram_message(msg)
else:
```

### Step 5: AWS Lambda Pe Deploy Karna (Bhai Ka Boss Move)

Ab ye script tere local VS Code mein toh chal gayi. Par ise cloud pe kaise daale?

1. **Dependencies Pack Karo:** AWS Lambda pe default mein Rasterio ya Numpy nahi hote. Tujhe ek "Lambda Layer" banana padega ya phir apne code ko zip karke saath mein libraries daalni padengi. 
   - Bhai, Linux pe `pip install rasterio -t .` chala ke us folder ko zip kar. 
   - Ya pre-built layers use kar (jaise klayers ya `geo-layer` for geospatial libs).

2. **Handler Function:** Lambda ko ek entry point chahiye. Tere code ko wrap kar:
```python
import json
import datetime

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    try:
        # Yahan upar ka pura code aayega
        # GEE Init, Data Pull, NDVI, Alert
        
        return {
            'statusCode': 200,
            'body': json.dumps('Alert system ran successfully!')
        }
    except Exception as e:
        # Error aaye toh telegram pe error bhej
        send_telegram_message(f"❌ Script crash huya bhai: {str(e)}")
        return {
            'statusCode': 500,
            'body': json.dumps(f'Error: {str(e)}')
        }
```

3. **EventBridge / Cron Trigger:** AWS Lambda pe jaa, "Add Trigger" click kar, aur "EventBridge (CloudWatch Events)" select kar. 
   - Rule type: Schedule
   - Cron expression: `0 4 * * ? *` (UTC time 4 AM, India time 9:30 AM).
   
Bas! Ab tera system set hai. Tu apne girlfriend/boyfriend ke saath movie dekh raha hai, aur tabhi tera phone buzz karta hai: "🚨 ALERT: PED KAAT RAHE HAIN!" 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** GEE requests thodi slow hoti hain. AWS Lambda ka default timeout 3 seconds hota hai. Saale, 3 second mein toh tu Google bhi nahi khol sakta. Lambda ke settings mein timeout ko kam se kam 2 minute (120 seconds) aur memory 512MB ya 1GB kar de. Nahi toh "Task timed out" error aayega aur tera dimaag kharab hoga.

Agar tujhe alert nahi, balki pura processed GeoTIFF chahiye daily download karne ke liye, toh Lambda pe mat chala. Bhai, Lambda ke paas time nahi hai itna. Uske liye GEE ka apna batch export use kar.

## Asli Funda (Core Concepts)

### Folium: Python Ka Magic Wand
Bhai, dekh. Folium aise samajh, jaise tu Swiggy pe biryani order karta hai. Tu Swiggy app pe button dabaata hai "Order Karo", aur background mein kya hota hai — restaurant ko notification jaata, delivery boy assign hota hai, payment gateway trigger hoti hai — tu woh sab nahi dekhta. Tu sirf "Order Placed" aur hot biryani dekhta hai. 

Folium bhi exactly aisa hai. Pehle se bana hua Leaflet.js (JavaScript ka ek badass mapping library) hai. JavaScript likhna saala dimag kharab kar deta hai. Brackets, semicolons, kya maa ch*d raha hai sacchi mein. Folium tujhe Python likhne deta hai, aur background mein wo JavaScript ka code generate karke browser ko serve karta hai. Tu Python likhega, browser interactive map dega. Jugaad level: Sachin Tendulkar ka straight drive.

### Coordinate System Ka Khel
Folium ko sirf ek cheez chahiye — Latitude aur Longitude. WGS84 coordinate system, jise humne pehle `EPSG:4326:` ke naam pe pehchaana tha. 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Saale, tu apna data UTM Zones (jaise EPSG:32644) mein chhod dega aur Folium mein daal dega, toh map pe tera deforestation hotspot Antarctica mein chala jaayega. "Bhai main Amazon jungle track kar raha tha, ye penguin kahan se aa gaya?" wali situation ho jaayegi. `Rasterio` aur `Numpy` se data nikalte hi, coordinates ko WGS84 (Lat/Lon) mein convert kar, tab hi Folium ko feed karna.

### Heatmaps: Data Ka Hulla Bol
Agar tere paas 500 deforestation points hain, toh un sab pe marker lagayega toh map ekdum bina kele ke jungle jaisa lagega. Pura mess. 

Isliye Heatmap use karta hai. Bhai ye aise samajh, jaise Mumbai ki garmi mein tapri pe chai ka bada khada ho. Jitne zyada log ek jagah khade hain, utni zyada garmi aur tension wahaan feel hoti hai. Bina ek shabd kahe, tera map chillata hai: "Yahan maa-behen ek ho rahi hai, yahan jungle saaf ho raha hai!"

### Step 2: Markers Lagao, "Kahaan Kaatwa?"
Ab apne detected points pe lal colour ke markers lagao. Hum circular markers use karenge, kyunki default wale pin (teer jaisa) thoda basic lagta hai.

```python
# Pichle chapter se aaya hua data. Coordinates aur change percentage.
# Ye list tere GEE account se ya Rasterio se nikal ke aayi hai.
deforestation_points = [
    {"coords": [-3.46, -62.21], "loss": "15% forest lost"},
    {"coords": [-3.50, -62.25], "loss": "22% forest lost"},

]

        location=point["coords"],

        color="red",

        fill_color="red",

m.save("deforestation_with_markers.html")
```

```python
            }
```

### Step 5: Raster Overlay (Agar Tujhe Image Hi Dikhani Hai)
Bhai, kabhi kabhi points nahi, pura satellite image hi map pe daalni padti hai. Jaise tu ne `Median Compositing` ki hai aur ek saaf image banayi hai jisme laal colour mein deforestation dikhta hai. Us image ko (GeoTIFF) map pe kaise daale?

Folium direct GeoTIFF nahi padh sakta. Tujhe usko pehle tile format mein convert karna padega. Iska asli jugaad hai: `branca` aur `rasterio` ka combination, ya seedha Earth Engine ka API use karke image ka map ID nikalna.

Kyunki humne `Earth Engine Account:` aur `Authentication:` pehle se set up kiya hua hai, hum GEE ka `ee.TileLayer` ko Folium mein daal sakte hain. Ek custom function bana lete hain iske liye, warna tu GEE ka official Python docs dekh ke rona suru kar dega.

```python
import ee

# Earth Engine initialize kar (Authentication wala step pichle chapters se)
ee.Initialize()

# Ek dummy image le rahe hain (NDVI loss wala area)
# Maan lo ye tera processed image hai jisme loss areas highlight hain
image = ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2") \
            .filterDate('2023-01-01', '2023-12-31') \
            .filterBounds(ee.Geometry.Point([-62.21, -3.46])) \
            .median()

# Visualization parameters. Yahan tune colors set kiye
vis_params = {
    'bands': ['SR_B4', 'SR_B3', 'SR_B2'], # RGB bands
    'min': 0,
    'max': 3000,
    'gamma': 1.4
}

# Map ID generate kar
map_id_dict = ee.Image(image).getMapId(vis_params)

# Folium tile layer bana ke add kar
folium.raster_layers.TileLayer(
    tiles=map_id_dict['tile_fetcher'].url_format,
    attr='Map Data © Google Earth Engine',
    overlay=True,
    name='Landsat Composite 2023'
).add_to(m)

folium.LayerControl().add_to(m)
m.save("deforestation_raster_overlay.html")
```

Samjha? Tu GEE se image laaya, uska URL nikala, aur Folium mein as a background tile daal diya. Ab map pe tera processed satellite image aa gaya, aur upar se tere deforestation markers bhi dikh rahe hain. Ekdum James Bond level ka spy gadget ban gaya tera dashboard.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** Bhai, yahan 80% log atak jaate hain. Kyunki coordinate system mismatch ho jaata hai. GEE default mein WGS84 deta hai, jo Folium ke liye perfect hai. ### Important Concepts Recap

Chal, ek chota sa revision le le, taaki concepts dimag mein solid rahein. 

1.  **Raster Data Handling:** Satellite images are not normal JPGs. Wo rasters hain, with multiple bands. Rasterio ka use karke tune in bands ko read, write, aur transform karna seekha. `src.read()` se data nikalna aur metadata preserve karna yeh sab kaam aaya.

2.  **Numpy Operations:** Numpy arrays ke bina kuch nahi hota. Tune array math, masking, aur calculations ki. NDVI calculate karte waqt `(NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)` ka formula Numpy ke through efficiently run kiya. Broadcasting aur vectorized operations ki power ab tujhe pata hai.

3.  **Cloud Masking:** QA bands ya cloud mask layers ka use karke tu ne invalid pixels ko `NaN` ya zero set kiya. Yeh crucial hai kyunki baadal ke peeche ped katne ka scene chhut sakta hai. 

4.  **Vegetation Indices:** NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) aur EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index) ki calculation. EVI is better in dense canopy areas, yeh tune seekha. In indices ki range (-1 to 1) ka interpretation karna, yeh skill tere paas hai.

5.  **Change Detection:** Do dates ke NDVI images ko subtract karke ya threshold apply karke tu ne deforestation spots identify kiye. Binary masks banaye aur difference map generate kiya.

6.  **Automation:** Script ko cron job ya cloud function pe daalne ki soch. API calls automate karna aur alerts trigger karna — yeh real-world application ka core tha.

7.  **Visualization:** Data sirf numbers nahi, dikhna chahiye. Folium ke saath interactive maps banaye, jahan user zoom karke exact location dekh sakta hai.

## Glossary

**Authentication**: A one-time browser-based verification process required to connect a local Python environment to Google Earth Engine.

**Cloud Masking**: The process of identifying and removing cloud and shadow pixels from satellite imagery using QA or SCL bands to ensure clean data.

**CRS (Coordinate Reference System)**: A coordinate-based framework used to define the exact spatial location and projection of raster data on a map.

**EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index)**: An advanced vegetation index that builds upon NDVI by incorporating blue band corrections to better handle atmospheric haze and canopy background.

**GEE (Google Earth Engine)**: A cloud-based geospatial processing platform providing access to petabytes of satellite data and computational power.

**GeoTIFF**: A standard image file format that includes embedded spatial metadata, allowing the raster data to be mapped accurately.

**ImageCollection**: A Google Earth Engine data structure used to store, filter, and process a stack of multiple satellite images.

**NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)**: A mathematical formula using Near-Infrared (NIR) and Red bands to quantify vegetation health and density.

**Numpy**: A fundamental Python library used to perform mathematical and logical operations on multi-dimensional arrays.

**QA Bands**: Quality Assurance bands in satellite datasets that contain pixel-level metadata regarding atmospheric conditions, clouds, and sensor reliability.

**Rasterio**: A Python library used for reading, writing, and manipulating geospatial raster data like GeoTIFFs.

**Sentinel-2**: A European satellite mission providing high-resolution, multi-spectral imagery commonly used for vegetation and land monitoring.

**Time-Series Composite**: A clean, synthesized image created by calculating the median of multiple masked satellite images over a specific time period.

**Virtual Environment**: An isolated Python workspace that manages project-specific dependencies and packages separately from the system's global setup.
